match_info.result: MI won by 6 wickets (with 5 balls remaining)

innings1_matchups.verdict: Thakur dominated Rahane — held him below his season average SR
Allen's SR crashed against Thakur — the slower ball was unplayable
Bumrah contained Raghuvanshi to 140 SR — 36 below his innings average
Rinku punished Ghazanfar — 225 SR against the spinner was his best passage

innings2_matchups.verdict: Rohit absolutely feasted on Arora before the dismissal — 233 SR
Chakravarthy was KKR's best option vs Rickelton — held him 31 below average
Narine had no answer for Rohit — 275 SR against the off-spinner

turning_points.label: Turning Point 1: The Thakur Triple
turning_points.description: Shardul Thakur's three wickets in four overs (Allen at 5.2, Green at 8.5, Rahane at 13.3) dismantled KKR's platform. At 69/0 after 5.2 overs, KKR were on track for 230+. Thakur's first over changed the trajectory completely.

turning_points.label: Turning Point 2: The Rickelton-Rohit Blitz
turning_points.description: MI's opening stand of 148 from 71 balls. By over 6, MI had 80/0 chasing 221. The KKR bowling attack had no answer. Rickelton hit 8 sixes; Rohit was at 50 off 23 balls.

turning_points.label: Turning Point 3: Wickets in Overs 14-15
turning_points.description: MI lost Suryakumar (14.2) and Rickelton (15.2) in consecutive overs, going from 179/1 to 184/3. KKR sensed a chance — but MI still only needed 37 from 5 overs with 7 wickets in hand. The game was never truly in doubt again.

counterfactual_scenarios.title: What If Shardul Thakur Had Bowled Average?
counterfactual_scenarios.change: Replace Thakur's 3/39 with an average IPL death/middle-overs spell of 0/48 (economy 12)
counterfactual_scenarios.narrative:
Shardul Thakur's first over wasn't supposed to happen like that. An average IPL replacement across those same four overs — bowling at 12 RPO and taking no wickets — adds roughly 17 extra runs and preserves all three dismissed batsmen. Finn Allen, playing at 217.64 SR when dismissed at 5.2, was just warming up. Cameron Green, caught miscuing off a pace-variation at 180 SR, had another 10 overs of batting left in him.

In the counterfactual, KKR reach the drinks break at 13 overs with Rahane still at the crease and 145/0 on the board. The Rahane-Raghuvanshi partnership that was worth 36 in reality becomes worth 60+, as Rahane no longer faces a scoring-rate imperative. With the keeper and batter cycling freely, KKR close at 237 or 238 — a target MI's top order could not have absorbed without a perfect powerplay.

The numbers quantify what the eye suspected: Thakur's three-wicket haul wasn't just match-altering, it was match-defining. Take it away, and you're looking at a 17-run swing and a KKR victory built on platform cricket rather than the collapse narrative we witnessed.

counterfactual_scenarios.title: What If Rohit Sharma Had Been Run Out at 50?
counterfactual_scenarios.change: Remove Rohit Sharma's 78-run contribution after ball 23 (when he reached 50). He scores 52 before being run out, batting 23 balls total.
counterfactual_scenarios.original_outcome: MI 224/4 in 19.1 overs — won by 6 wickets
counterfactual_scenarios.lola_outcome: MI 195/5 in 19.1 overs — lost by 25 runs (counterfactual target 221)
counterfactual_scenarios.narrative:
Rohit Sharma at his best in T20 cricket doesn't just score runs — he reframes the asking rate so aggressively that opposition bowlers lose their plans. By the end of over 6, Rohit was on 51 off 23 balls. The 50% win probability that existed at the toss had already blown out to 75% for MI.

Imagine instead a run-out at that exact moment — a direct hit from Anukul Roy that didn't bounce off his knee guard in reality. Rohit departs for 52. MI are 100/1 at over 8, with Rickelton still batting (he hit all 8 of his sixes regardless). But now the asking rate is 9.3 from 12 overs, not the 6.5 it was in reality.

The Rickelton run-out that followed in reality came when MI were 184 chasing 221 with enormous cushion. In our scenario, it comes when MI are 160 and need 61 off 7 overs — a coin-flip requiring clean hitting rather than clinical execution. KKR win by 25 runs in a match where one direct hit changed everything. This is why Rohit's powerplay isn't just an aesthetic pleasure — it's structural insurance.

commentary_gems.text: lord strikes! Allen looks to go downtown but ends up dragging this slower delivery to wide long-on. KKR were running away with it, so this is a big wicket. That was bowled at 117kph.
commentary_gems.context: Shardul Thakur's first wicket — the turning point of KKR's innings

commentary_gems.text: Shardul Thakur can do no wrong today! Three overs, three wickets! Rahane falls with the pressure building. Cross-seam full ball outside off.
commentary_gems.context: Completing the Thakur hat-trick of wickets

commentary_gems.text: Anukul Roy hangs on to a clanger! He has a habit of those! 137kph full ball angled across, Rohit looks to slog it away but it comes off the top edge.
commentary_gems.context: Rohit's dismissal — the catch nobody expected Roy to hold

commentary_gems.text: Finds the gap! Hardik Pandya levels the scores as he takes on this half-tracker. Pulls it away wide of long-off, and MI are one run away
commentary_gems.context: The penultimate ball — MI needed 2 off 2

commentary_gems.text: And Mumbai Indians win their first season-opener since 2012! And they do it by pulling off their biggest chase!
commentary_gems.context: The winning moment — Roy to Naman Dhir, FOUR

game_changers.name: Shardul Thakur
game_changers.contribution: Thakur's three wickets transformed a KKR innings that was cruising at 69/0 after 5.2 overs into a 220-all-out. At 217.64 SR, Finn Allen was batting in another gear — the dismissal at 5.2 changed the match's centre of gravity entirely. Player of the Match, and correctly so.

game_changers.name: Rohit Sharma
game_changers.contribution: Rohit's 78 off 38 in the powerplay dismantled the chase before it began. KKR's bowlers had no answer. By over 6, MI were 80/0 and the required rate had dropped below 10. The innings wasn't won in the last over — it was won in the first six.

game_changers.name: Ryan Rickelton
game_changers.contribution: Eight sixes from the wicketkeeper-batter announced an IPL career with force. Rickelton's contribution at the other end of the Rohit stand wasn't just runs — it was sustained pressure on every bowler. His run-out at 184 was the only moment KKR could celebrate.

game_changers.name: Angkrish Raghuvanshi
game_changers.contribution: From the losing side: Raghuvanshi's 51 off 29 in the death overs is the reason KKR posted 220 rather than 190. After Rahane fell at 145/3 in over 13, Raghuvanshi absorbed the pressure and then detonated — 50 runs in the last 5 overs with Rinku alongside him.
